EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually rich monsoonal moisture was in place on the morning of August 26th, with precipitable water close to 1.00 inch across eastern Utah and western Colorado. A jet streak moving in aloft brought not only additional moisture, but some extra lift as well. In addition, daytime heating on the complex terrain in this region help to spur on afternoon convection. As a result, widespread shower and thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon, producing localized very heavy rain and high rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 5:20 PM MDT, heavy rain in the area north of Mexican Hat, UT, resulting in failed culverts and flash flooding. On State Highway 163, the failed culverts and and heavy rain resulted in water over the road, making them impassable until the water receded. Additionally, heavy rain in this region led to water over the road on County Highway 261, in an area of steep switchbacks. While no damage was reported, these roads did become impassable and had to be cleared in the hours after the water receded. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection on the afternoon of September 30th was very isolated over eastern Utah and western Colorado, though coverage was slightly better over the higher terrain of the eastern Uinta Mountains and the Tavaputs. There was a 60 knot jet streak passing by aloft that, when tapped into by either deep mixing or a stronger thunderstorm, could bring stronger wind gusts down to the surface. For the most part, this led to breezier conditions with widespread gusts of 30 mph to 40 mph, but for some storms in the eastern Uinta Basin, this allowed for gusts into the upper 50s mph and low 60s mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:50 PM MDT, the mesonet site PC353 Vernal West recorded a wind gust to 58 mph. This was associated with a passing thunderstorm that also produced a wind gust to 48 mph at the KVEL ASOS at Vernal Airport, on the other side of town. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported standing water on Interstate 70 near Cisco, UT due to heavy rain from passing thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bryson Canyon RAWS station recorded a gust to 60 mph in association with a passing line of thunderstorms. To the south, the KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport recorded a gust to 54 mph in association with this same line. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were reported with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved through the Great Basin into the Four Corners region on the heels of another system. This low brought deep Atmospheric River moisture from the Desert Southwest through the Four Corners ahead of it, along with impressive dynamics and a strong jet to produce moderate to heavy snowfall across most mountain ranges of eastern Utah and western Colorado where the highest amounts of 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the mountains of eastern Utah and southwest Colorado. Temperatures remained mild enough that most lower valleys saw primarily rain, limiting snowfall accumulations.  Winds were quite strong as well with gusts in the mountains in the 60 to 80 mph range on average, leading to impacts from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 20 to 24 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 50 mph range with a peak wind gust of 70 mph at Little Mountain on March 6 at 15:40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system was approaching eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west on the 6ht, bringing mountain snow and valley rain showers. In addition, as this low approached, bringing with it a potent cold font, south to southwesterly winds strengthened through the day. Much of the region saw wind gusts 40-50 mph, with a few gusts reaching 60-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust of 64 mph was recorded at the KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, approximately 10 miles north of Moab, UT. This gust occurred at approximately 4:00 PM MST. Gusts across the region ranged from 45 mph to the peak gust of 64 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As is often the case, this high wind event was precipitated by an approaching strong low pressure system, with an associated strong upper level jet and cold front helping to enhance south to southwesterly winds out ahead of it. In addition, showers that formed along and ahead of the cold front helped to produce enhanced gusts across the lower desert valleys. Wind gusts of 45-55 mph were common across the lower elevations, with a few stronger gusts recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, about 10 miles north of Moab, UT, recorded two wind gusts to 61 mph on the afternoon of the 18th. The first occurred at 2:15 PM MDT, and the second at approximately 3:00 PM MDT. These gusts were likely enhanced by a passing shower associated with the cold front, as no strong gusts were recorded after this. However, prior to these peak gust, recorded gusts across the zone ranged from 45-55 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally around 20 inches of snow fell across the area at East Willow Creek. A peak wind gust of 49 mph occurred at Upper Sand Wash RAWS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with 14 to 16 inches of snow at the Abajo Mountains and 10 to 20 inches of snow at the La Sal Mountains. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 50 mph range with a peak wind gust of 52 mph at Monticello Airport. Wind gusts were likely higher at mountain peak level but the observations are missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front across the area that moved through from northwest to southeast. Light to moderate snowfall fell over most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with heaviest amounts favored among the Uncompahgre Gorge of the northwest San Juans as well as some northwest favored slopes. The northwest Colorado valleys also saw light to moderate snowfall with lesser amounts elsewhere. In addition strong winds led to impacts due to blowing snow and low visibility with gusts in the 60 to 80 mph range across some higher mountain areas and gusts of 40 to 60 mph across the northwest Colorado valleys in addition to the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds generally gusted in the 35 to 45 mph range with a peak wind gust of 48 mph at Windy Point on March 31 at 22:10. During the April portion of the event, the peak wind gust at Windy Point was 46 mph at 2:40 on April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold front and shortwave trough moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The higher amounts fell across the mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado due to favorable orographics and better available moisture. In addition, strong winds led to blowing snow across the mountain areas. Light to moderate snowfall fell across some valley locations including portions of the southwest Colorado valley foothills as well as central mountain corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area with half the area receiving a foot or more. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 60 mph range with a peak wind gust of 63 mph at UT43W Stateline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold front and shortwave trough moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The higher amounts fell across the mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado due to favorable orographics and better available moisture. In addition, strong winds led to blowing snow across the mountain areas. Light to moderate snowfall fell across some valley locations including portions of the southwest Colorado valley foothills as well as central mountain corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 17 inches of snow fell across the area with 8 to 10 over the Abajo Mountains and 12 to 17 over the majority of the La Sal Mountains. A peak wind gust of 48 mph occurred at Monticello, with wind gusts likely higher at mountain peak level but observations are missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough and associated cold front brought mild air ahead of it and much colder air behind it to result in light to moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with rain changing over to snow in many lower valley locations even though accumulations remained light. Heavier snowfall seemed to favor the La Sal Mountains of southeast Utah as well as the northwest San Juans and Grand Mesa mainly due to the colder northerly flow behind the cold front. Winds were also fairly strong leading to blowing and drifting snow in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 4 to 5 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains. Winds gusted in the 50 to 55 mph range with a peak wind gust of 57 mph at Monticello. Wind gusts were likely higher at mountain top level but observations are missing.

This was due primarily to heavy rainfall on already saturate soils. Impacts were minimal and once the rain stopped, the roadway cleared.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1024 MST on September 12, public reported 0.75 inch diameter hail in Moab, Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved into the region resulting in a winter storm, characterized by a more mild southwesterly flow period initially which later on transitioned to a colder northwesterly flow. This event brought substantial snow accumulations to Utah's mountains, with some lighter accumulations noted at many lower elevation spots. Additionally, an avalanche in the western Uinta mountains resulted in the fatality of a backcountry skier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Beaver Dams, Pickle Keg, and Oak Creek recorded from 1.0 to 2.3 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 27 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved into the region resulting in a winter storm, characterized by a more mild southwesterly flow period initially which later on transitioned to a colder northwesterly flow. This event brought substantial snow accumulations to Utah's mountains, with some lighter accumulations noted at many lower elevation spots. Additionally, an avalanche in the western Uinta mountains resulted in the fatality of a backcountry skier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of a skier who failed to return home by 500 pm MST was received by Summit County Dispatch on the evening of the event 3/7. A search and rescue operation was started that evening, and crews found evidence of a recent slide, but due to reportedly extremely hazardous conditions the crews had to wait to until the following morning. Search and continue the search rescue crew ultimately found the body of a deceased 51 year old man at 1049 am MST, with a determination that he had been caught in the avalanche on a steep northeast aspect of Hoyt Peaks NE bowl. A forecaster with the Utah Avalanche Center stated that warm and strong spring sunshine may have been a contributing factor to reactivate multiple weak layers buried beneath the current snowpack. A nearby SNOTEL, Beaver Divide, recorded 1.1 inches of water from the start of the event through 500 pm MST 3/7, which at a reasonable 13 to 1 snow to liquid ratio would equate to 14.3 inches of snow. The closest SNOTEL, Redden Mine LWR, through the same time period recorded 2.5 inches of water, or at the same ratio approximately 32.5 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Mill-d North, Cascade Mountain, and Timpanogos Divide recorded from 1.4 to 2.8 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 19 to 38 inches of snow. Additionally, many snow safety community partners, including those within the Utah Department of Transportation, observed snow water equivalent ranging from approximately 1.8 to 2.8 inches, resulting in 21 to 27 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave trough and a subsequent period of colder northwesterly flow brought accumulating snow and impacts to portions of Utah's mountains as well as in portions of the Salt Lake Valley. On the front side of this system, a period of strong winds were observed in several locations across western Utah, especially with and directly after frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of strong post-frontal winds were observed in terrain adjacent locations in Castle Country. The Emery County EM reported that in the town of Emery 2 houses lost roofing materials, 1 house lost its roof, and in one location approximately 50 ft of vinyl fence was blown over. The EM also reported that there were overturned semi trucks on I-70 in this time period, likely due to the strong winds. The MUDDY CREEK NEAR EMERY 4N mesonet station recorded a peak gust of 57 mph at 1:15 PM MST. The FREMONT JUNCTION mesonet station recorded a peak gust of 73 mph at 11:30 PM MST, and also observed several hours of winds roughly gusting in the 60-70 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp trough resulted in a potent winter storm impacting Utah, largely in two separate waves with a break between them. The first wave was within the prefrontal and milder southwesterly, which brought more of the high elevation snowfall. A reinforcing secondary wave moved through after the front within a cooler northwesterly flow, dropping snow levels and resulting in valley impacts, though at levels below warning/event amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Panguitch Lake Rs, Gutz Peak, and Kolob recorded from 1.0 to 2.3 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 30 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave trough and a subsequent period of colder northwesterly flow brought accumulating snow and impacts to portions of Utah's mountains as well as in portions of the Salt Lake Valley. On the front side of this system, a period of strong winds were observed in several locations across western Utah, especially with and directly after frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement in addition to the Utah Avalanche Center reported a natural avalanche in Little Cottonwood Canyon between Snowbird and Alta, which resulted in the partial burial of 3 vehicles containing a total of 5 or 6 passengers. No injuries were reported, and the main canyon road was reopened at approximately 7:30 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp trough resulted in a potent winter storm impacting Utah, largely in two separate waves with a break between them. The first wave was within the prefrontal and milder southwesterly, which brought more of the high elevation snowfall. A reinforcing secondary wave moved through after the front within a cooler northwesterly flow, dropping snow levels and resulting in valley impacts, though at levels below warning/event amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Timpanogos Divide, Mill-D North, and Atwater recorded from 1.6 to 2.4 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 21 to 32 inches of snow. Additionally, snow safety partners including those at Alta, Brighton, and Park City reported approximately 1.6 to 3.0 inches of snow water equivalent, with corresponding snow measurements 23 to 39 inches.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave trough and a subsequent period of colder northwesterly flow brought accumulating snow and impacts to portions of Utah's mountains as well as in portions of the Salt Lake Valley. On the front side of this system, a period of strong winds were observed in several locations across western Utah, especially with and directly after frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of high winds accompanied a frontal passage into western Utah. The first high wind gust was observed at 7:10 PM MST at the I-80 @ mp 1 mesonet station with a gust of 64 mph. The highest wind gusts recorded occurred at the Wendover Port of Entry mesonet station at 7:15 PM MST and at the I-80 @ mp 29 mesonet station at 7:40 PM MST, with gust magnitudes at both peaking at 67 mph. The final high wind gust was recorded at the Wendover Peak mesonet station at 9:30 PM MST with a gust of 61 mph. Several other mesonet stations observed high wind gusts within this period as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave trough and a subsequent period of colder northwesterly flow brought accumulating snow and impacts to portions of Utah's mountains as well as in portions of the Salt Lake Valley. On the front side of this system, a period of strong winds were observed in several locations across western Utah, especially with and directly after frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous public and spotter reports of snowfall accumulations between 2 to 9 inches were received throughout the day. Given the onset time of snow, adverse to dangerous driving conditions were observed during the morning commute hours particularly, with numerous accidents and slide-offs reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive shortwave trough and a subsequent period of colder northwesterly flow brought accumulating snow and impacts to portions of Utah's mountains as well as in portions of the Salt Lake Valley. On the front side of this system, a period of strong winds were observed in several locations across western Utah, especially with and directly after frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Cascade Mountain, Mill-D North, and Atwater recorded from 1.6 to 2.6 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 19 to 34 inches of snow. Additionally, many snow safety community partners, including those within the Utah Department of Transportation, observed snow water equivalent ranging from approximately 1.2 to 2.1 inches, resulting in 18 to 32 inches of snow. Finally, high winds were also observed at some of the Higher Peaks of the Wasatch such as Hidden Peak and Mount Baldy, with a peak gust of 102 mph recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front ushered in an arctic airmass on the evening of January 17th, as well as bringing widespread wind and snow to eastern Utah and western Colorado. This arctic airmass was reinforced over the course of the next week by several passing waves with their own associated cold fronts. Temperatures during this period were 15-20 degrees below normal, and in some places close to 30 degrees below normal. With temperatures this cold, any amount of wind would produce a dangerously cold wind chill. The coldest period of this event was January 20th to the 22nd, with temperatures finally beginning to moderate after the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, near Moab, recorded an approximately 1 hour period where temperatures were below 25F and wind chill values were below 15F. Winds were below 10 mph but still produced sufficiently cold wind chill values. Temperatures ranged from 18F to 25F and winds ranged from 6 mph to 8 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded was 8F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KBDG AWOS in Blanding recorded an approximately 1.5 hour period of temperatures below 25F and wind chill values below 15F. Winds were below 10 mph but still produced sufficiently cold wind chill values. Temperatures ranged from 21F to 23F and winds ranged from 6 mph to 7 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded was 13F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the back end of a modest winter storm, lingering precipitation (with some lake enhancement likely) resulted in impacts to transportation and commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per Utah Highway Patrol, a crash involving 14 vehicles occurred, closing down all northbound lanes of I-15 in Spanish Fork for at least 30 minutes, after which point some flow of traffic resumed. One driver was reportedly transported to a hospital in stable condition. Light snow showers associated with the final portion of a winter system were ongoing around this time, likely with some periods of lake induced enhancement. Nearby, the Spanish Fork Municipal Airport AWOS (KSPK) reported deteriorated conditions ahead of the incident, with observations nearest the time of the incident indicating snow and freezing fog with visibility of 0.25 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of cold weather helped form ice on freezable bodies of water across the area, with a subsequent warmup leading to more instability on ice. Late in the month, two boys fell through ice, resulting in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two boys fell through ice into Glasmann Pond in South Ogden while fishing, approximately around 4:30 pm LST. While one was able to get out, the other was rescued by divers and later passed from the injuries sustained. Temperatures nearby reported from the Ogden-Hinckley Airport ASOS (KOGD) around the time of the incident were 39F, with clear skies reported. The maximum temperature that day (1/27) was 40F, the prior day (1/26) was 40F, and one more day prior (1/25) was 35F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While several avalanches were noted throughout the month of January given the tenuous nature of the snowpack, one in the later portion of the month resulted in injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sometime during the day, a party of backcountry skiers started a day in Big Cottonwood Canyon, eventually working their way up to the Park City ridgeline. The skiers then planned a descent into the Katie's area. One of the skiers started a descent, causing a hard slab to break approximately 30 ft above them on the second turn of their run. This avalanche caused the skier to be pulled into a chute in the woods, and carried the skier an estimated 500 ft. The skier was able to extract themselves from the snow, and while a life flight had been called, the skier reportedly only suffered minor injuries and significant bruising, and emergency services deemed to be not needed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty south to southwest winds were in place ahead of a cold front, along with gusty north to northwest winds behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at the SR-201 at I-80 mesonet site. Other wind gusts include 67 mph at the I-80 at MP 79 Timpie mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty south to southwest winds were in place ahead of a cold front, along with gusty north to northwest winds behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the CW3996 Oak City mesonet site. Other wind gusts include 62 mph at the US-6 @ Eureka mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty south to southwest winds were in place ahead of a cold front, along with gusty north to northwest winds behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at the Point of the Mountain mesonet site. Other wind gusts include 59 mph at the EW4925 Provo mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river event brought mountain snow and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 63 mph was recorded at the Vernon Hill mesonet site. Other wind gusts include 59 mph at the SR-201 at I-80 mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snowmobiler got caught in an avalanche in the Monte Cristo Snowmobile Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler got caught in an avalanche in the Monte Cristo Snowmobile Area near the Rich and Cache county line. The victim's friend called for help, prompting a multi-agency search in which the victim was found deceased around 6:20 p.m. Monday, February 3. Deputies reported the snow was very deep where the avalanche occurred and it took 4 hours to locate the victim. Prior to this event, the northern Utah mountains received 1-2 feet of snow between Friday, January 31 and Sunday, February 2, followed by rising temperatures and strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One skier was injured and another was killed by an avalanche in the Silver Fork area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier and a guide were caught in an avalanche in the East Bowl of upper Silver Fork. The skier triggered the avalanche above the guide who was skiing below. Both were carried by the avalanche. The skier was badly injured, with a broken leg, but survived. The guide had traumatic injuries and died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved into the Great Basin, bringing a surge of strong Atmospheric River moisture on the order of 200 to 300 percent of normal ahead of it. Some colder air moved in after trough passage as the flow shifted from southwest to northwest, changing rain over to snow in lower elevations. Strong dynamics and high moisture content resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snowfall in most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The La Sal and Abajo Mountains benefitted the most with heavy snowfall on the order of 1 to 2 feet. In terms of the lower elevations, the southeast Utah valleys benefitted the most from the southwest flow moderate accumulations while the remaining lower valleys of eastern Utah saw minimal accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 57 mph range at elevations at or above 8,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures and clear skies promoted deep mixing on the 7th of February. In the meantime, the pressure gradient aloft was tightening ahead of a cold and strong low pressure system approaching the area from the west, leading to strong winds at upper levels. The unusual warmth of the day promoted deep mixing, tapping into those strong upper level winds and bringing strong gusts down to the surface. Higher elevations and the valleys of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado saw the peak gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vernal West mesonet station recorded a peak gust to 60 mph at 220 PM MST on February 7th. This was the only gust above 58 mph. Gusts around the region ranged from 45 mph to 55 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

Other gusts recorded in the area during this period were two gusts to 53 mph at the Utah Department of Transportation Mesonet site I-70 At Mp 226 Harley Dome. One gust was at 110 PM MST and the other was at 330 PM MST. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Utah and western Colorado. As precipitation began to dwindle over southwestern Colorado, dense fog began to develop in areas that had received fresh snow. These patches of dense fog were isolated and transient, with sensors going in and out of dense fog conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, near Moab, recorded an approximately 1.5 hour period of reduced visibility. The area had received precipitation in the hours prior, and as skies tried to clear out and temperatures cooled, fog developed. Visibility only dropped below 0.5 mile for about 15 minutes, but was below 1 mile for the whole period. That said, this fog was isolated to the area around the airport, and therefore this was not a missed Dense Fog Advisory. A significant winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Utah and western Colorado. As precipitation began to dwindle over southwestern Colorado, dense fog began to develop in areas that had received fresh snow. These patches of dense fog were isolated and transient, with sensors going in and out of dense fog conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Eastern Uinta Basin had received some snow in the hours before dense fog developed on February 21st. However this fog was fairly transient, and the dense fog period lasted less than half an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KVEL ASOS in Vernal recorded an approximately 1 hour period where visibility was reduced due to dense fog. This fog was transient, and dense fog with visibility of 1.4 mile or less lasted for about 20 minutes, while the remainder of the period saw visibility under 1 mile. After 10AM MST, the fog lifted. Due to the short period of dense fog and the localized nature, this does not count as a missed Dense Fog Advisory.  No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of August continued where July left off, with overall above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation. In particular, the lower elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado showed the greatest departures in both temperatures and precipitation. As a result, Extreme (D3) Drought either remained unchanged in coverage, or expanded through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of August, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering roughly 75% of Utah Zone 023 - Eastern Uinta Mountains. These conditions remained unchanged through the end of the month of August.

In particular, the lower elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado showed the greatest departures in both temperatures and precipitation. EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of August, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering less than 10% of Utah Zone 024- Eastern Uinta Basin, mainly along the Colorado border. With the 8/20/25 US Drought Monitor update, the area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions was expanded slightly, but still comprised less than 10% of the zone. Conditions remained unchanged from the 8/20/25 update through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Park Service employees at Arches National Park reported Cache Valley Road had washed out near the lower Delicate Arch viewpoint.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of August continued where July left off, with overall above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation. In particular, the lower elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado showed the greatest departures in both temperatures and precipitation. As a result, Extreme (D3) Drought either remained unchanged in coverage, or expanded through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of August, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions that covered roughly 1/3 of Utah Zone 025 - Tavaputs Plateau, mainly across the far western edge of the zone. With the 8/20/25 US Drought Monitor update, the area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions was expanded to cover roughly 2/3 of the zone. From the 8/20/25 update through the end of the month, conditions remained unchanged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2:30 PM MDT, the mesonet site US-191 at MP 104 Flat Iron recorded a wind gust to 60 mph in association with a passing thunderstorm. Other sensors in the area recorded wind gusts of 45-55 mph in association with the same storm. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received in association with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving into the Pacific Northwest led to the upper level pressure gradient to begin tightening over the eastern Great Basin, leading to increasing winds aloft. At the same time, high pressure still in place over eastern Utah and western Colorado helped promote deep daytime mixing that allowed those strong upper level winds to mix down to the surface. This resulted in widespread wind gusts of 40-50 mph, with gusts upwards of 60 mph in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vernal CWOP site on the west side of the city of Vernal recorded two wind gusts at or above 58 mph. The first gust, to 58 mph, occurred at 1:20 PM MDT, and the second gust, to 72 mph, occurred at 6:10 PM MDT. These were the only two gusts to reach this strength in the area on this day. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough dropping out of the Pacific Northwest on the 20th helped to tighten the pressure gradient aloft. This in turn worked to increase surface winds across eastern Utah and western Colorado. In addition, a ridge of high pressure that was still partly in place on the 20th promoted clear skies and deep mixing, helping to mix down even stronger winds to the surface. This pattern lingered into the afternoon of the 21st, allowing for a few late wind gusts to pop up at higher elevations of northwest Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A falling tree branch that killed one person was likely a result of gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Perry Police Department was called to Perry Park just before 5 PM Wednesday, June 11 for reports of traumatic injuries due to a large tree branch falling on two individuals. One individual, an 80-year old woman, died on the scene. The other individual, a 28-year old woman, was transported to the hospital in serious condition. There were no thunderstorms in the vicinity of Perry during this time, but strong thunderstorms capable of producing strong outflow winds were ongoing over southeast Idaho. Outflow winds from those distant thunderstorms produced wind gusts in the 30-40 mph range, but it is unknown if the strong winds were the direct cause of the tree branch falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth wildfire in Washington County affected residences in the Pine Valley Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth Fire started on Thursday, June 19th, 2025. Initial reports from fire officials state the fire is suspected to have originated as a holdover from lightning in the Pine Valley Mountains. A long stretch of hot, dry, and windy conditions prevailed ahead of the fire's discovery on the 19th. A nearby RAWS station in Pine Valley (TT900) observed Red Flag conditions (wind gusts >25 mph and RH <15%) for about 16 hours from the afternoon of the 19th to the early morning hours on the 20th. A mandatory evacuation order for the Pine Valley community was issued at 9:21 PM on the 19th. Red Flag conditions redeveloped late morning on the 20th, with strong southwest winds eventually pushing the fire into the community of Pine Valley. Washington County Sheriff reports 14 homes and 4 outbuildings destroyed in Pine Valley despite mitigation efforts. Red Flag conditions continued June 21st with the TT900 RAWS station reporting wind gusts upwards of 44 mph with RH in the single digits to teens. This resulted in the fire spreading to the north side of the valley and making a run at the communities of Grass Valley and Pinto, which were placed in an evacuation order at 5:37 PM on June 21st. Evacuation orders were lifted at 10 AM Friday, June 27. A total of 15,563 acres were affected by the wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 20 vehicle crash on southbound U.S. 89 in Davis County was followed by a 9 vehicle crash on the northbound lanes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utah Highway Patrol troopers reported a crash occurred shortly after 6:30 a.m. on the southbound side of U.S. Highway 89 in Fruit Heights. All 20 vehicles involved were headed south when the pileup occurred. Authorities said drivers were traveling too fast for the slick, snow-covered roads. Around the same time, a separate crash involving nine vehicles occurred on the northbound side of the highway. Troopers stated weather was a contributing factor in this crash as well, and some drivers may have lost control while looking toward the earlier southbound pileup. At the time of the event, light snow had created accumulations in spots on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As is often the case with high wind events across eastern Utah and western Colorado, this event was driven by an approaching low pressure system that was tightening the pressure gradient aloft ahead of it. A potent jet streak was moving through the southwesterly flow ahead of this system as well, and with a dry streak moving into the region, deep mixing during the day on the 17th was anticipated to tap into the stronger winds of this jet and mix them down. A third contributing factor to the strong winds on the 17h was the approaching cold front, which had stalled out over northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, driving strong southwesterly surface winds out ahead of it. All together, this event led to widespread gusts of 45-55 mph across eastern Utah and the lower elevations of western Colorado, with several gusts of 60 mph or better.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCNY ASOS located at Canyonlands Airport, about 10 miles north of Moab, UT, recorded a wind gust to 69 mph at approximately 5:30 PM local time on the 17th. Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to the peak gust of 69 mph. These winds lasted through the afternoon and into the early evening before beginning to diminish as the cold front moved through. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a strong spring storm that pushed a potent cold front through the area, clear skies and calm winds allowed temperatures to drop below freezing across much of eastern Utah. This cold air stuck around for a few days, with good radiational cooling conditions allowing for several nights with below normal temperatures, particularly across southeast Utah. With several periods of much above normal temperatures punctuated by near-normal temperatures, the growing season in the area was well underway. Therefore a widespread freeze at this time of the year could have a detrimental effect on fruit crops and other agriculture in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest temperature recorded across the Southeast Utah zone, as well as the lowest temperature recorded at the KBDG AWOS in Blanding, UT, was 20F. A total of 9 out of the 11 available temperature sensors in the zone recorded at least 3 hours of temperatures below 32F. The average time below 32F was 8 hours, with a maximum of 13 hours and a minimum of 0 hours. Additionally, temperatures across the zone were at or below 28F for on average 4 hours, with a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 0, qualifying this as a hard freeze event. However, no reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature recorded at the KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, about 10 miles north of Moab, UT, was 21F, which was also the lowest temperature recorded at any of the sensors available in the zone. A total of 17 out of the 18 available sensors recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with an average duration of 6 hours, a maximum of 12 hours and a minimum of 2 hours. The average duration of temperatures at or below 28F was only 2.5 hours, which does not qualify this event as a hard freeze, however some locations saw up to 9 hours of temperatures at or below 28F. No reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Under clear skies and light winds, temperatures across southeast Utah dropped below 32F again on the night of April 3rd to the 4th. The KBDG AWOS in Blanding, UT, recorded a minimum temperature of 26F, while the minimum at any available sensor was 25F. A total of 10 out of the available 11 sensors recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with an average of 7.5 hours, a maximum of 12 hours, and a minimum of 2 hours. Temperatures were at or below 28F for an average of 2 hours, which does not qualify this event as a hard freeze, as that threshold is 3 hours. However, some locations did experience up to 10 hours of temperatures at or below 28F, leading to localized hard freeze conditions. No reports of crop damages or loss were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front across the area that moved through from northwest to southeast. Light to moderate snowfall fell over most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with heaviest amounts favored among the Uncompahgre Gorge of the northwest San Juans as well as some northwest favored slopes. The northwest Colorado valleys also saw light to moderate snowfall with lesser amounts elsewhere. In addition strong winds led to impacts due to blowing snow and low visibility with gusts in the 60 to 80 mph range across some higher mountain areas and gusts of 40 to 60 mph across the northwest Colorado valleys in addition to the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds generally gusted in the 35 to 45 mph range with a peak wind gust of 48 mph at Windy Point on March 31 at 22:10. During the April portion of the event, the peak wind gust at Windy Point was 46 mph at 2:40 on April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the middle of April, most lower elevation forecast zones had hit the 50 growing degree day thresholds, meaning that the growing season was well underway. Much of this was due to a period of above normal temperatures the week prior. However, a strong spring storm with a potent cold front moved through the area from the 17th to the 18th, bringing in much colder air. As skies cleared and winds dropped on the night of the 18th into the morning of the 19th, temperatures began to plummet across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature recorded at the KVEL ASOS at Vernal Airport was 21F, while the minimum recorded at any of the available 22 sensors was 18F. All of the 22 available sensors recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with an average duration of 10.5 hours, a maximum of 16 hours and a minimum of 5 hours. Additionally, temperatures were recorded at or below 28F for an average of 6.5 hours, with a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 1 hour. Therefore this event qualifies as a hard freeze as well. No reports of crop losses or damages were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front across the area that moved through from northwest to southeast. Light to moderate snowfall fell over most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with heaviest amounts favored among the Uncompahgre Gorge of the northwest San Juans as well as some northwest favored slopes. The northwest Colorado valleys also saw light to moderate snowfall with lesser amounts elsewhere. In addition strong winds led to impacts due to blowing snow and low visibility with gusts in the 60 to 80 mph range across some higher mountain areas and gusts of 40 to 60 mph across the northwest Colorado valleys in addition to the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 2 to 5 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mudslide reached the road in Spanish Fork Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mud slide happened on U.S. 6 in Spanish Fork Canyon. No vehicles were involved, but both directions were closed for several hours for clean up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two individuals went out on the water at Willard Bay State Park on an inflatable raft during the afternoon of Saturday, August 2. Outflow winds from thunderstorms near the Utah-Idaho border pushed through Willard Bay. The winds threw two people off an inflatable raft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two individuals went out on the water at Willard Bay State Park on an inflatable raft during the afternoon of Saturday, August 2. Outflow winds from thunderstorms near the Utah-Idaho border pushed through Willard Bay area around 4:20 p.m. with wind gusts at nearby weather stations reported to be as high as 25-30 mph with the initial pass of the boundary. Winds remained elevated out of the northwest, with gusts upwards of 25-35 mph around 1.5 hours following the outflow boundary. The two individuals on the raft were reported to have been thrown off the raft when the boundary came through. One person resurfaced while the other did not.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system lifted northeast through the Four Corners, bringing enough moisture and lift to result in moderate to heavy snowfall across the southern mountains with light to moderate snow to the West Elk Mountains and much lighter amounts elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell across the La Sal Mountains where over a foot of snow fell. Wind gusts stayed below 45 mph overall in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 16 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 6 to 12 inches over the Abajo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and windy conditions occurred ahead of a strong cold front and associated low pressure trough that brought much colder temperatures, with rain changing over to snow in some valleys. Moderate to heavy snow fell across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with the southern mountains receiving heavier snowfall in excess of a foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 9 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 50 mph range with a peak wind gust of 50 mph at Windy Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and windy conditions occurred ahead of a strong cold front and associated low pressure trough that brought much colder temperatures, with rain changing over to snow in some valleys. Moderate to heavy snow fell across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with the southern mountains receiving heavier snowfall in excess of a foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 12 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 3 to 7 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains. 6 inches of snow was reported at Monticello 0.4 SW. Winds gusted in the 45 to 55 mph at the onset of the event with a peak wind gust of 57 mph at Big Indian RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously deep moisture was present across southeast Utah and southwest Colorado on the afternoon of June 4th. Enough sunshine had gotten through during the morning hours to create afternoon instability of 500-1000 J/Kg, more than enough to produce some strong storms. In addition, atmospheric profiles favored heavy rain development and slow moving, training thunderstorms. All these factors combined to lead to a few instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Taylor Canyon on the afternoon of June 4h. This flooding eroded a six food embankment along White Rim Road in Canyonlands National Park, and carried sediment all the way to the Green River. Additionally, several washes along Taylor Canyon Road ran and washed out sections of Taylor Canyon Road. No other damages, and no injuries or fatalities, were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture, peak heating, instability and enough shear was present to produce another round of strong storms despite no distinct disturbance moving through.  One particular storm 10 miles south of Moab produced a landspout tornado as enough shear allowed this to form.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landspout tornado developed over Flat Iron Mesa, about 10 miles south of Moab in open country. It was first observed from cloud to ground at 1030 MST and lasted about 15 more minutes before their view was obstructed. The landspout appeared to kick up dirt but didn't seem to be any damage. It was relatively stationary and viewed from a distance. Time was based on radar estimates coupled with spotter report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bridge Creek Fire started in mid June due to a lightning strike on the north side of Navajo Mountain, which is about 55 miles northwest of Page, Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bridge Creek Fire started in mid June due to a lightning strike on the north side of Navajo Mountain, which is about 55 miles northwest of Page, Arizona. The fire grew to 2,588 acres before it was contained in mid to late July. The estimated final cost was $10 million. This fire is located in San Juan County, Utah near or along the border with WFO SLC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved into the Great Basin, bringing a surge of strong Atmospheric River moisture on the order of 200 to 300 percent of normal ahead of it. Some colder air moved in after trough passage as the flow shifted from southwest to northwest, changing rain over to snow in lower elevations. Strong dynamics and high moisture content resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snowfall in most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The La Sal and Abajo Mountains benefitted the most with heavy snowfall on the order of 1 to 2 feet. In terms of the lower elevations, the southeast Utah valleys benefitted the most from the southwest flow moderate accumulations while the remaining lower valleys of eastern Utah saw minimal accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 25 inches of snow fell across the area with 15 to 20 inches over the La Sal Mountains with 24 to 25 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains. 12 inches of snow was reported at Monticello 0.4 SW in the lower elevations. Wind gusts stayed below 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of July 15th, several showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain surrounding the Uintah Basin, in which the city of Vernal is located. As these storms underwent their lifecycles and began to decay, they sent out gusty outflow boundaries that raced through the Basin, producing gusts to nearly 60 mph. With drought conditions ongoing, there was plenty of loose soil and dust to kick up, resulting in localized dust storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KVEL ASOS at Vernal Airport in Vernal, UT recorded sustained winds 25-40 mph, with gusts upward of 60 mph. Thanks to ongoing drought conditions, there was plenty of loose soil and dust to be picked up by strong winds like these, and indeed the KVEL ASOS recorded a half hour period of visibility reduced below 1/2 mile. No reports of impacts due to this dust storm were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak northern stream shortwave trough moving through the northwest CONUS resulted in the development of high based convection. Some of this convection resulted in strong to severe wind gusts. Additionally, a cutoff low placed off of the southwest CONUS helped advect moisture into southern Utah, leading to convection with periods of sufficiently heavy rain to result in flash flooding over some rain sensitive areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water in Kanarra Falls required several hikers to be rescued. Time is estimated  based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of July saw above normal temperatures and generally below normal precipitation across eastern Utah and western Colorado. What little moisture did manage to move into the region favored the higher terrain, allowing drought conditions across the lower elevations to worsen through the month. A brief period of moisture at the beginning of the month did contribute to brief but significant drought improvements to certain areas, but the continued hot and dry month after this point led to expansion of extant drought conditions and the return of drought to areas where drought had improved significantly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With worsening drought conditions through the month of July, Extreme (D3) Drought finally expanded into northeastern Utah for the first time at the end of the month. The area of Extreme (D3) Drought was rather small, and surrounded by Severe (D2) Drought conditions. No reports of damages to crops or other issues related to drought conditions were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of July saw above normal temperatures and generally below normal precipitation across eastern Utah and western Colorado. What little moisture did manage to move into the region favored the higher terrain, allowing drought conditions across the lower elevations to worsen through the month. A brief period of moisture at the beginning of the month did contribute to brief but significant drought improvements to certain areas, but the continued hot and dry month after this point led to expansion of extant drought conditions and the return of drought to areas where drought had improved significantly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With hot and dry weather continuing through the month of July, drought conditions worsened across northeast Utah. By the end of the month, areas of Extreme (D3) Drought were analyzed across the eastern Uinta Basin, surrounded by Severe (D2) Drought conditions. No reports of crop damages or failures, or other drought-related impacts were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Deer Creek Fire started in early July due to human causes 1 mile north of Old La Sal, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deer Creek Fire started in early July due to human causes 1 mile north of Old La Sal, Utah. The fire grew to 17,724 acres and spread into portions of west-central Colorado particularly western Montrose county, before it was contained in early August. The estimated cost to date was $24,021,755. 11 private structures, a U.S. Forest Service guard station and communication site were destroyed in the fire shortly after it started. A fire induced tornado rated EF2 also occurred on the Deer Creek Fire on July 12, with wind speeds up to 122 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bridge Creek Fire started in mid June due to a lightning strike on the north side of Navajo Mountain, which is about 55 miles northwest of Page, Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bridge Creek Fire started in mid June due to a lightning strike on the north side of Navajo Mountain, which is about 55 miles northwest of Page, Arizona. The fire grew to 2,588 acres before it was contained in mid to late July. The estimated final cost was $10 million. This fire is located in San Juan County, Utah near or along the border with WFO SLC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large expansive of high pressure in place with triple digit heat. Extreme fire behavior on the Deer Creek Fire resulted in pyrocumulus to develop over the fire with conditions leading to the development of a fire-induced tornado. This fire-induced tornado occurred at the Deer Creek Fire near La Sal, Utah on July 12th in the early afternoon. The tornado was rated an EF2 with a path of 100 yards wide and winds estimated at 122 mph, lasting approximately 12 minutes. Storm surveys indicated extensive roof damage to a home and several outbuildings were also damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire-induced tornado occurred at the Deer Creek Fire near La Sal, Utah on July 12th. The tornado was rated an EF2 with a path of 100 yards wide and winds estimated at 122 mph, lasting approximately 12 minutes. Storm surveys indicated extensive roof damage to a home and several outbuildings were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 806 MST on September 12, an NWS employee reported 1 inch diameter hail 25 miles NNW of Castle Valley, Utah. Some hail stones a little bigger than one inch fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest was slowly pulling moisture into the region from the west with several shortwave disturbances moving across the north. This along with diurnal heating resulted in a few thunderstorms with strong gusty winds as the low levels remain very dry. One particular storm over the Eastern Uinta Mountains produced outflow winds up to 59 mph at Vernal Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm over the Eastern Uinta Mountains produced outflow winds up to 59 mph, which was a measured peak wind gust at the Vernal Regional Airport at 1444 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of September continued to run above normal in terms of temperatures, and below normal in terms of precipitation. However, there were a few periods of heavy rain across the region, particularly around September 12th, and again at the end of the month, which helped to improve drought conditions across the region. A dry end to the month did allow for some worsening drought around the Four Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of September, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions as covering roughly 10% of Utah Zone 024 - Eastern Uinta Basin, with the remaining 90% under Severe (D2) Drought conditions. With the 9/17 Drought Monitor update, all areas of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions were removed, with 100% of the zone under Severe (D2) Drought conditions. No substantial changes in drought conditions occurred between this point and the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of severe thunderstorms moved across a portion of southeast Utah which generated two confirmed tornadoes in San Juan County, Utah on Saturday, Sept 13, 2025. The first tornado was a quick spin-up and very short lived, as noted by a resident's video footage narration, along with information from local emergency management and law enforcement. ||A second, more well developed tornado, developed five minutes after the first tornado dissipated and formed in a location near the first tornado. The second tornado was well documented by local officials and video footage to have remained nearly stationary throughout its duration before finally dissipating. Both events were located north of Montezuma Creek. ||The entire event lasted around one hour and 20 minutes, with the first tornado |beginning around 12:30pm and the second tornado dissipating by 1:50pm MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second tornado prompted a damage survey conducted by local emergency|management and Navajo Nation emergency management, along with local law enforcement. The data from the survey was relayed to the National Weather Service: storm damage was concentrated on an area centered near 37.33071N / 109.3259W and included; a home completely destroyed, two (2) 800-gallon water barrels were missing, a three-bedroom home was completely destroyed, a sheep corral and barn were destroyed, two (2) horse trailers were damaged, a cow corral, along with damage to three (3) vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked to the north of the region during the day on the 16th, with an associated front draped across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. An upper level jet streak was tracking across region during this period as well, providing some extra lift. Instability in the realm of 600-800 J/kg of CAPE was also present across this region, as well as ample moisture. All these combined to fire off strong to severe thunderstorms across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado during the afternoon hours. A few storms in northeast Utah produced some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1322 MST on September 16, a trained spotter reported 1 inch diameter hail 9 miles ESE of Whiterocks, Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of September continued to run above normal in terms of temperatures, and below normal in terms of precipitation. However, there were a few periods of heavy rain across the region, particularly around September 12th, and again at the end of the month, which helped to improve drought conditions across the region. A dry end to the month did allow for some worsening drought around the Four Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of September, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions as covering roughly 75% of Utah Zone 023 - Eastern Uinta Mountains, with the remaining 25% under Severe (D2) conditions. With the 9/17 Drought Monitor update, the coverage of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions was reduced to 70% of the zone. With the 9/24 update, the coverage of Extreme (D3) Drought was reduced to 50% of the zone. From this point to the end of the month, no substantial changes to drought conditions occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry end to September and a dry start to October kept drought conditions consistent across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado at the start of the month. However, the arrival of deep tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond helped to substantially improve drought conditions, especially across southwest Colorado. In this region, drought was completely eradicated. Only portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where the deep tropical moisture did not reach, saw drought conditions persist more or less unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of October, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering roughly 60% of Utah Zone 023 - Eastern Uinta Mountains, with the remaining 40% under Severe (D2) Drought conditions. With the 10/14 Drought Monitor update, Extreme (D3) Drought coverage was reduced to 40%, with 60% of the zone under Severe (D2) Drought. No substantial changes were noted between this point and the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a several day period caused flash flooding for much of the Tooele Valley. Rainfall totals through the event ranged from 2-4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tooele Co. EM reported a Union Pacific Railroad track washed over/out from water near Droubay Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were strong winds throughout northern Utah followed by rain and lightning for some places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Redwest Music Festival, being held at the Utah State Fairpark, was paused and crowds were evacuated preemptively around 5 p.m. Saturday due to a cold front approaching the area. It is estimated that 60,000 people attended each day of the three day event. The cold front brought wind gusts from 35-50 mph at the nearest sensors starting around 5:15 p.m. Wind gusts in that range continued through around 7 p.m. Following the cold front, thunderstorms tracked into the area, bringing lightning and moderate to heavy rain. Thunderstorms diminished around 8 p.m. with rain through around 9:30 p.m. The event was called off around 9:30 p.m. due to unsafe conditions. The event remain paused with the fairpark evacuated through then. News reports say that a woman was taking shelter in a vehicle parked near the fairpark at 1055 W. North Temple St. Some reports say she was in the vehicle and had exited, while others say she had evacuated the festival and was standing outside the vehicle. A large plank from scaffolding of a nearby construction site was blown off, striking the woman and the vehicle. The woman died from the injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry end to September and a dry start to October kept drought conditions consistent across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado at the start of the month. However, the arrival of deep tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond helped to substantially improve drought conditions, especially across southwest Colorado. In this region, drought was completely eradicated. Only portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where the deep tropical moisture did not reach, saw drought conditions persist more or less unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of October, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions as covering roughly 1% of the zone, with 70% of the zone under severe (D2) Drought conditions, and the remaining 29% under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. With the 10/14 Drought Monitor update, Extreme (D3) Drought was completely removed, with 95% of the zone now under Severe (D2) Drought conditions, with the remaining 5% under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. With the 10/21 update, Severe (D2) Drought conditions were reduced to 15% coverage, with Moderate (D1) Drought conditions now covering 85% of the zone. No significant changes were noted between this point and the end of the month.

With the 10/14 update, significant improvements were noted. Extreme (D3) Drought conditions were reduced to 5% coverage, with Severe (D2) Drought increased to 95% of the zone. The 10/21 update saw the introduction of Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. The coverage of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions remained unchanged, but Severe (D2) Drought was reduced to 30% of the zone, with Moderate (D1) Drought now covering roughly 65% of the zone. No significant changes were noted from this point through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry end to September and a dry start to October kept drought conditions consistent across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado at the start of the month. However, the arrival of deep tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond helped to substantially improve drought conditions, especially across southwest Colorado. In this region, drought was completely eradicated. Only portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where the deep tropical moisture did not reach, saw drought conditions persist more or less unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of October, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering roughly 5% of Utah Zone 028 - La Sal and Abajo Mountains, with the remaining 95% under Severe (D2) Drought conditions. Significant improvements were noted with the 10/14 Drought Monitor update. Extreme (D3) Drought conditions were removed and Moderate (D1) Drought conditions were introduced. Severe (D2) Drought conditions now covered roughly 75% of the zone, with the remaining 15% under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. With the 10/21 update, Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions were introduced. Severe (D2) Drought was reduced to 20% coverage, with Moderate (D1) Drought covering 50% of the zone, and the remaining 30% under Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. No substantial changes were noted between this point and the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of October, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought covering roughly 15% of Utah Zone 029 - Canyonlands and Natural Bridges, with the remaining 85% under Severe (D2) Drought. With the 10/14 Drought Monitor update, Extreme (D3) Drought coverage was reduced to 10%, Severe (D2) Drought was reduced to 50% coverage, and Moderate (D1) Drought was introduced to cover the remaining 40% of the zone. Extreme (D3) Drought coverage remained unchanged, but Severe (D2) Drought was reduced to 40%, Moderate (D1) Drought was reduced to 10%, and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions covered the remaining 40%. There were no significant changes noted between this point and the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front descended through eastern Utah and western Colorado in the late afternoon and evening of the 27th. By nighttime, northwesterly flow had established itself behind the frontal boundary, dropping temperatures to below freezing. One Utah zone experienced a season-ending freeze during this pattern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All twenty-one reporting stations available for verification recording temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, and sixteen of those twenty-one recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least three hours. This means that at least 50% of the zone experienced a season-ending freeze event. The lowest temperature recorded was 15F, while the lowest recorded at the KVEL ASOS in Vernal was 25F. Minimum temperatures ranged from 15F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted for on average 8 hours, with a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 4. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted for on average 4 hours, with a maximum of 9 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was more than four inches of rain in some places that resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was more than four inches of rain in portions of northern Washington County, with a water rescue at Baker Dam, flooded roads, and flooding of homes in Brookside.||A group of four tourists were in a van and did not realize they were driving on to a dam. When first responders arrived, there was around a foot of water in the vehicle. They were able to rescue everyone, and no injuries were reported.||The road into Pine Valley was blocked for a time with debris and water, along with some damage to a newly constructed culvert at Meadow View and Main St.||Black Ridge Road and Old Highway 91 in the New Harmony area were closed because the reservoir that is usually dry filled up.||There was also a lot of water in Brookside. Several homes got water in basements and were very close to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of November was overall warmer than normal across eastern Utah and western Colorado, but was wetter or drier than normal depending on where you stood. As a result, only minor changes to drought of Severe (D2), Moderate (D1), or Abnormally Dry (D0) categories occurred with each update, but the area of Extreme (D3) Drought remained unchanged through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought rain and snowfall to the Abajo mountains and surrounding areas on November 23. Overnight, the trough moved out of the region. A weak pressure gradient made for calmer winds, and a drying mid-level atmosphere cleared skies. With the recent precipitation though, low-level moisture remained pooled over southeastern Utah leading to the development of patchy dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed near the Abajo mountains throughout the night of November 23-24. The KBDG AWOS at the Blanding Municipal Airport reported multiple hours of 1/4-mile visibility due to fog and freezing fog early on the morning of the 24th. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

The KU64 AWOS at the Monticello City Airport reported over five hours of reduced visibility due to freezing fog prior to midnight. However, 1/4-mile visibility was only briefly reached three times during this period, lasting no longer than 20 minutes each time. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A drier mid-level atmosphere set up across the region on the afternoon of November 24 behind a departing shortwave trough. Strong pools of low-level moisture remained over portions of southeastern Utah though due to prior rainfall on November 23. As a result, the environment here remained saturated for most of the day, eventually prompting very brief and localized dense fog just after sunset before the low-level pool of moisture dried out overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed near the Abajo mountains early in the night of November 24. The KBDG AWOS at the Blanding Municipal Airport reported about one hour of 1/4-mile visibility due to fog just after sunset. The low-level pool of moisture supporting fog development dried up quickly after, limiting the fog's duration and potential impacts from reduced visibility. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough and associated cold front dropped southward out of the Pacific Northwest across the Great Basin and through eastern Utah and western Colorado. Moderate to heavy snowfall favored the central and southern mountains with higher amounts along the frontal boundary draped across the central mountains, La Sal Mountains and San Juan mountains. Gusty winds in the 45 to 55 mph range above 12,000 feet also contributed to blowing snow with the forcing from the jet dynamics and cold frontal boundary with banded snow as well.  Most lower valleys remained limited with snowfall due to higher snow levels with exception of the Paonia to Hotchkiss area that benefited from banding along the front and convection helping drive snow levels lower at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 12 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains with 2 to 5 inches of snow across the Abajo Mountains. Winds gusted up to 56 mph at Abajo Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough impacted portions of the forecast area and carried enough cold air in addition to moisture to lower snow levels to yield isolated lower elevation impacts. While amounts were well below warning criteria (even for mountains), and likely even advisory for low elevations, given the holiday weekend impacts were potentially increased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utah Highway Patrol noted a large increase in motor vehicle accidents as a likely result of weather. While not everywhere in the Salt Lake Valley saw snow, let alone accumulating snow, some locations especially along the benches experienced periods of poor conditions. For example, a Utah Department of Transportation RWIS reported periods of deteriorated conditions from around sunrise to early afternoon, with peak snowfall rates near 2 per hour, visibility at times to near 1/4 mile (and frequently below 2 miles), and periodic slush-ups on I-215.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough descending from the Pacific Northwest brought a round of snow showers to portions the Intermountain West. While precipitation over the Uinta Mountains was minimal, low-level moisture still rose with the passage of the low. As the attendant cold front moved through near midnight on December 1, fog began to develop on the northern slopes of the Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed on the northern slopes of the Uintah Mountains late in the night on December 1. The K40U AWOS at the Manila Airport reported multiple hours of 1/4-mile visibility early in the morning and beyond sunrise due to freezing fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly weak cutoff low slowly ejected northward through the area over the course of several days. This feature in combination with sufficient moisture created an environment that supported several days of shower and thunderstorm development. Some of these storms produced locally heavy rainfall and flash flooding, as well as some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson Spring mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 60 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.
